videos.label: Percentage Aptitude Tricks for Placements — Lecture 01
videos.note: PrepInsta — TCS / Infosys / Wipro focused

learnIt.realLife: Your friend scored 45 out of 50 in one test and you scored 72 out of 80 in another. Who did better? The totals are different, so the raw marks cannot be compared straight away. Percentages fix this by putting everything on the same scale — out of 100 — so any two things instantly become comparable.
learnIt.plainMeaning: The words 'per cent' literally mean 'out of 100'. So 30% simply means 30 out of every 100. If 30% of a class are girls, then for every 100 students, 30 are girls. A percentage is really just a fraction that always has 100 on the bottom.
learnIt.mathIntro: Because percent means 'out of 100', you can move between a fraction and a percentage any time: to turn a fraction into a percent, multiply by 100; to turn a percent into a fraction, divide by 100.

formulas.label: What percent one number is of another
formulas.breakdown: Part is the piece you have, Whole is the full amount, and × 100 turns the fraction into an out-of-100 number. If 45 of 50 marks are yours: (45 ÷ 50) × 100 = 90%.

formulas.label: A percentage of a number
formulas.breakdown: Divide the percent by 100 to make it a fraction, then multiply by the number. 20% of 150 = (20 ÷ 100) × 150 = 30.

formulas.label: Percentage change
formulas.breakdown: Subtract the old value from the new to get the change, divide by the OLD value (always the starting point), then × 100. If a price goes from 200 to 250: (250 − 200) ÷ 200 × 100 = 25% increase.

steps.why: 'Per cent' means out of 100, so 25% is 25/100.
In maths, the word 'of' between a percent and a number always means multiply.
Turning 25% into the simpler fraction 1/4 makes the arithmetic much easier.

examples.problem: A student scored 45 out of 60. What percentage is that?

steps.why: The marks scored are the part; the maximum marks are the whole.
This formula turns '45 out of 60' into an out-of-100 figure.
First do the division inside the bracket.
Multiplying by 100 turns the decimal into a percentage.

examples.problem: A shop raises the price of a bag from ₹800 by 25%, then gives a 20% discount on the new price. What is the final price?

steps.action: Find the 25% rise on the starting price ₹800: 25% of 800 = (25/100) × 800 = 200.
steps.why: A percentage change is taken on the value you start from — here that is ₹800.
An increase is added to the original price.

steps.action: Now take the 20% discount on the NEW price ₹1000, not the old ₹800: 20% of 1000 = (20/100) × 1000 = 200.
steps.why: A discount is always calculated on the current marked price, which is now ₹1000.
A discount is taken away from the price it applies to.

examples.answer: ₹800 — the same as the start, because the two percentages were taken on different amounts.

commonMistakes: Taking the second percentage on the original amount. A 25% rise then a 20% fall is NOT 'up 5%' (25 − 20). Each percent sits on a different base, so you must apply them one after the other.
Mixing up 'percent' and 'percentage points'. Going from 20% to 25% is a rise of 5 percentage points, but that is a 25% increase in the rate itself.
In percentage change, dividing by the new value instead of the old one. Always divide by where you started.
Turning 'of' into a plus. '20% of 150' means 20/100 × 150, never 20 + 150.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Read it straight off: 'of' means × and 'per cent' means ÷ 100, so '20% of 150' is 20 ÷ 100 × 150. And memorise the friendly fractions — 10% = 1/10, 20% = 1/5, 25% = 1/4, 50% = 1/2, 12.5% = 1/8 — because swapping to the fraction makes the maths instant.

crackIt.theTrick: Turn every percentage into an easy fraction, and use the rule 'x% of y = y% of x' to flip the hard number onto the easy one.

shortcut.formula: x% of y = y% of x   •   friendly fractions (10%=1/10, 20%=1/5, 25%=1/4, 50%=1/2, 12.5%=1/8, 33.33%=1/3)   •   two successive changes: net % = a + b + (a × b)/100
shortcut.whereNumbersGo: Compute whichever side is easier: 16% of 25 is hard, but 25% of 16 = 16 ÷ 4 = 4 — same answer, far easier. For a rise of a% then a fall of b%, put a and b (use a minus sign for a decrease) into a + b + ab/100.

steps: net % = a + b + ab/100 with a = +25, b = −20

crackIt.whyItWorks: 'x% of y = y% of x' works because both are the very same multiplication: (x/100) × y equals (y/100) × x. The net-change formula works because the second percentage acts on the already-changed amount, and the extra ab/100 term captures that overlap.
whenWorks: Fraction swaps and the x%-of-y flip: any 'percent of a number' calculation.
The a + b + ab/100 formula: exactly two successive percentage changes.
whenNot: The net-change formula is for TWO changes only — for three or more, apply them one at a time.
Fraction shortcuts help only for the 'nice' percentages (10, 12.5, 20, 25, 33.33, 50). For an odd one like 37%, just compute directly.
crackIt.practicePattern: Any 'successive increase/decrease' or 'raise then discount' question is a net-change-formula question. Any 'find x% of a number' with a nice percentage is a fraction-swap question.

videos.label: Speed, Time & Distance — Basic to Advanced
videos.note: Basics to advanced solved questions

learnIt.realLife: You walk to school every day. Some days you walk fast and reach in 10 minutes; some days you stroll and it takes 20 minutes. The road never changed — only your speed and your time did. That single everyday fact is this whole topic: how fast you go and how long you take together decide how far you reach.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Speed just means how much distance you cover in one unit of time — like 'in one hour'. If a car covers 60 kilometres in 1 hour, its speed is 60 kilometres per hour (written 60 km/h). Distance is the total length you travelled. Time is how long it took. These three are joined together: if you know any two of them, you can always find the third.
learnIt.mathIntro: One simple relationship connects all three: distance equals speed multiplied by time. From this one idea we can rearrange the letters to find speed or time whenever we need to.

formulas.label: Distance
formulas.breakdown: Distance (how far you go) equals Speed (how far you cover in one hour) times Time (how many hours you travel). Going 50 km every hour for 3 hours gives 50 × 3 = 150 km.

formulas.label: Speed
formulas.breakdown: Speed equals the total Distance divided by the Time taken. Covering 150 km in 3 hours means each hour you did 150 ÷ 3 = 50, so your speed is 50 km/h.

formulas.label: Time
formulas.breakdown: Time equals the total Distance divided by the Speed. To cover 150 km at 50 km/h you need 150 ÷ 50 = 3 hours.

formulas.label: Changing units (km/h and m/s)
formulas.breakdown: When a question mixes kilometres-per-hour with metres-per-second, put them in the same unit first. To turn km/h into m/s multiply by 5/18; to go back, multiply by 18/5. So 36 km/h = 36 × 5/18 = 10 m/s.

examples.problem: A car travels at 40 km/h for 2 hours. How far does it go?

steps.action: Write down what you are given: Speed = 40 km/h, Time = 2 hours. We need the Distance.
steps.why: Listing the known values first stops you from mixing up which number is which.

steps.action: Pick the formula that gives Distance: Distance = Speed × Time.
steps.why: We already have speed and time and we want distance, so this is the matching form.
Replace the words with the values you just wrote down.
This is the actual calculation.

examples.problem: A train covers 240 km in 4 hours. What is its speed?

steps.action: Given: Distance = 240 km, Time = 4 hours. We need Speed.
steps.why: Note the known values before touching any formula.
We have distance and time and want speed, so we divide.
Swap the words for the values.
The arithmetic step.

examples.problem: A man drives from home to office at 40 km/h and returns on the same road at 60 km/h. What is his average speed for the whole trip?

steps.action: Do NOT just average 40 and 60. Average speed = total distance ÷ total time, so we must find the time each way first.
steps.why: Average speed always means total distance over total time — never the plain average of the two speeds.

steps.action: The distance each way is the same but unknown, so call it d km. Time going = d ÷ 40; time returning = d ÷ 60.
steps.why: Using a letter for the unknown distance lets us work even though the number is not given.

steps.action: Total distance = d + d = 2d. Total time = d/40 + d/60 = 3d/120 + 2d/120 = 5d/120 = d/24.
steps.why: You can only add the two times after giving them the same denominator (120).

steps.action: Average speed = 2d ÷ (d/24) = 2d × 24/d = 48. The d cancels out.
steps.why: Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its flip, and the unknown d disappears — so we never needed its real value.

commonMistakes: Averaging the two speeds directly. For a there-and-back trip at 40 and 60, the answer is 48, not (40 + 60) ÷ 2 = 50. Equal distances are NOT equal times, so the plain average is wrong.
Mixing units. If speed is in km/h then time must be in hours, not minutes. 30 minutes is 0.5 hour, not 30.
Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s (or back) when a question mixes them. Multiply by 5/18 to go from km/h to m/s.
Reaching for a formula before writing down what is given — that is how the wrong values get plugged in.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Draw a triangle with D on top and S and T in the two bottom corners. Cover the letter you want with your finger: cover D and you see S × T; cover S and you see D over T (D ÷ T); cover T and you see D over S (D ÷ S). This 'D-S-T triangle' hands you the right formula every time.

crackIt.theTrick: Stop juggling three formulas — use the D-S-T triangle for the basics, and for a trip covering the same distance at two speeds use the ready-made average-speed formula 2ab/(a+b).

shortcut.formula: D-S-T triangle (D on top; S and T below)   •   Average speed for equal distances = 2ab/(a+b)
shortcut.whereNumbersGo: For basics, cover the letter you want in the triangle and read off × or ÷. For a there-and-back (equal distance) trip, put the two speeds in as a and b into 2ab/(a+b).

steps: Cover D in the triangle → D = S × T
Cover S in the triangle → S = D ÷ T

examples.problem: Same road at 40 and 60 km/h — average speed?

comparison.normal: Call distance d, find each time (d/40 and d/60), add them with common denominator 120 to get d/24, then 2d ÷ (d/24) = 48. About 2 minutes.
Recall Distance = Speed × Time, substitute, multiply: 40 × 2 = 80. About 30 seconds.
comparison.shortcut: Cover D in the triangle → 40 × 2 = 80. About 5 seconds.

crackIt.whyItWorks: The triangle is just the single equation D = S × T rearranged three ways — covering a letter is algebra done with your finger. The 2ab/(a+b) formula works because you spend more time at the slower speed, which drags the average below the plain midpoint, and the formula already builds that in.
whenWorks: The triangle: any basic question giving you two of distance, speed, time and asking for the third.
2ab/(a+b): only when the two distances are EQUAL, like a go-and-return trip on the same road.
whenNot: Do not use 2ab/(a+b) when the two distances are different — go back to total distance ÷ total time.
Do not use any formula before fixing units — convert minutes to hours and km/h to m/s first.
crackIt.practicePattern: Any question that says 'goes at one speed and returns at another' or 'covers equal distances at two speeds' is a 2ab/(a+b) question. Any question that gives two of {distance, speed, time} and asks the third is a triangle question.
